Name: spectral_latency_optimization
Title: Spectral Latency Optimization for Training-Free Machine Learning
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that spectral methods can be used to optimize latency in training-free machine learning frameworks, leveraging the geometric properties of activations to dynamically allocate compute resources and enforce strict latency budgets.
Related Work: Our proposal builds upon recent works in training-free machine learning, including Spectral Quantization Routing and Geometric Signal Separation. However, our approach differs in its focus on spectral latency optimization, utilizing PAC-Bayes frameworks and information-theoretic bounds to establish a mathematically transparent and reproducible pathway for efficient inference.
Abstract: We propose a novel framework for spectral latency optimization in training-free machine learning, leveraging the geometric properties of activations to dynamically allocate compute resources and enforce strict latency budgets. Our approach utilizes PAC-Bayes frameworks and information-theoretic bounds to establish a mathematically transparent and reproducible pathway for efficient inference. We demonstrate the efficacy of our framework on single-GPU setups across diverse benchmarks, achieving a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic baselines.
Experiments: Evaluate the proposed framework on single-GPU setups across diverse benchmarks, including WILDS and ICL benchmarks.
Compare the performance of our framework to static and heuristic baselines, measuring accuracy retention, subspace alignment stability, and inference efficiency.

Conduct ablation studies to analyze the impact of spectral methods on latency optimization, including the effect of different spectral decomposition techniques and the role of PAC-Bayes frameworks in establishing mathematically transparent bounds.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed framework may require significant computational resources for spectral decomposition and PAC-Bayes bound computation.
The efficacy of our approach may depend on the quality of the spectral decomposition and the choice of hyperparameters.

The proposed framework may not be applicable to all types of machine learning models or tasks, and may require additional modifications or extensions to accommodate different architectures or domains.
